refined_fr.refined_fr: Enable measurement and reporting of material throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the system's capability to measure/report throughput metrics, and (2) the achievement of a customer-defined target. The latter is a success criterion dependent on operational execution, not a system requirement. The refined FR isolates the system's functional obligation—providing visibility into throughput—which is solution-neutral and testable independent of whether a specific productivity target is met. The target itself belongs in acceptance criteria or KPI definitions, not in the FR.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the NGHE solution captures and reports material throughput (m³/h or ton/h) during representative operations. Verify data accuracy against independent measurement. Confirm reporting is accessible to Site Manager and Equipment Operator in real time or near-real time.
sub_frs: Acquire volumetric or mass flow data from material handling subsystems during operation.
Calculate throughput rate (m³/h or ton/h) from acquired flow data.
Display or transmit throughput metrics to authorized stakeholders in real time or near-real time.
Log historical throughput data for post-operation analysis and reporting.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire instantaneous flow rate via continuous sensing of material stream properties (mass or volume per unit time) and integrate over evaluation period to compute cumulative throughput
DP2: Measure discrete material batches (count, mass, or volume per batch) at regular intervals and extrapolate to throughput rate over evaluation period
DP3: Monitor material accumulation in a calibrated reservoir or staging area (level, weight, or volume change) over time and derive throughput from rate of change
DP4: Combine multiple independent measurement modalities (e.g., batch counting + continuous sensing) with cross-validation logic to report throughput with confidence bounds
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1: Acquire instantaneous flow rate via continuous sensing of material stream properties (mass or volume per unit time) and integrate over evaluation period to compute cumulative throughput

potential_sources: Sensor placement and material stream geometry: continuous sensing requires stable, representative sampling point; poor placement couples measurement accuracy to process layout
Calibration drift over evaluation period: if sensor calibration is not maintained independently, throughput reporting becomes coupled to sensor aging
Integration method and time-base synchronization: if clock or integration logic is tightly bound to a single data acquisition module, recalibration or replacement becomes difficult
Environmental interference: if sensing modality is sensitive to temperature, humidity, or electromagnetic noise, measurement becomes coupled to worksite conditions not under system control
coupling_check.notes: DP1 is logically decoupled from specific sensing technology (mass flow, volumetric flow, or proxy methods). However, realization (IM layer) must isolate sensor acquisition, calibration management, and integration computation into separate modules to maintain independence. Recommend explicit separation: (IM-Sensor) acquires raw signal, (IM-Calibration) manages reference standards, (IM-Integrator) performs time-series aggregation.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Material stream properties are measurable and stable during representative operations. Risk: Highly variable material density, moisture content, or flow regime may degrade sensor accuracy below acceptance threshold.

Assumption: Evaluation period is long enough to average transient effects and sensor noise. Risk: Short evaluation windows or high-frequency disturbances may cause reported throughput to fall below productivity_target due to measurement noise rather than actual process underperformance.

Assumption: Sensor calibration can be maintained or verified independently during the evaluation period. Risk: If calibration drift is not detected, reported throughput may be systematically biased, violating acceptance criteria.

Assumption: Worksite safety plan permits installation and operation of continuous sensing equipment in the material stream. Risk: Safety constraints (e.g., explosive atmosphere, confined space) may prohibit certain sensing modalities, forcing fallback to DP2 or DP3.

Assumption: Integration method (e.g., numerical summation, Riemann sum) is appropriate for the time-resolution and variability of the flow signal. Risk: Coarse time-sampling or aliasing may introduce systematic error in cumulative throughput.

Assumption: Reported throughput is compared against productivity_target using a consistent unit and time-base. Risk: Unit conversion errors (e.g., mass vs. volume, per-hour vs. per-shift) could cause false acceptance or rejection.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to specify: (1) acceptable measurement uncertainty or tolerance band relative to productivity_target (e.g., ±5%), (2) time-resolution and evaluation period duration, (3) material property ranges (density, particle size, moisture) expected during representative operations, (4) environmental conditions (temperature, humidity, electromagnetic noise) at the worksite, and (5) whether safety plan permits continuous in-stream sensing or requires non-intrusive methods. Additionally, clarify whether 'reported productivity ≥ productivity_target' means instantaneous rate or cumulative average, and whether the acceptance criterion is deterministic or probabilistic (e.g., 95% confidence). These refinements will constrain DP selection and enable rigorous IM design with clear module boundaries for sensor, calibration, integration, and reporting.

im_boundary.name: Vehicle
im_boundary.scope: Physical and logical boundary encompassing all onboard sensing, actuation, and real-time data acquisition systems including flow rate measurement hardware, sensor integration, signal conditioning, and cumulative throughput computation

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle transmits instantaneous flow rate and cumulative throughput measurements to external systems (NGHE Solution, Teleoperation) for monitoring and logging

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle receives evaluation period parameters and flow measurement configuration commands from Teleoperation or NGHE Solution

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle communicates flow data and measurement status over network to support remote monitoring and control

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment: continuous sensing hardware, signal acquisition, integration logic, and cumulative computation are all vehicle-resident subsystems. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry output and tasking input cross boundary; core measurement physics remains internal.

Poor containment: NGHE is a high-level orchestration/planning layer. Flow sensing hardware and real-time acquisition must remain on Vehicle. Integration and cumulative computation logic would be split between Vehicle (hardware) and NGHE (software), creating tight coupling and latency issues.

Poor containment: Teleoperation is a remote command/monitoring interface, not a sensing platform. Continuous sensing and integration require local, real-time execution on the Vehicle. Placing DP logic in Teleoperation introduces unacceptable network latency and loses sensor fidelity.

Very poor containment: Safety Services is a cross-cutting concern for hazard detection and mitigation, not a measurement acquisition system. Flow rate sensing is orthogonal to safety logic. Placing DP here violates separation of concerns and creates unnecessary coupling.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Vehicle module is the natural owner of instantaneous flow rate acquisition and cumulative throughput computation because: (1) sensing hardware (flow meters, pressure transducers, mass sensors) are physically mounted on the vehicle; (2) real-time signal conditioning and integration require low-latency local execution; (3) the measurement physics (continuous sensing → integration over period) is a core vehicle subsystem function, not a planning, teleoperation, or safety concern; (4) external systems (NGHE, Teleoperation) consume the output via Telemetry interface, maintaining clean separation.
containment_risks: Risk: If flow measurement requires external calibration or reference data from NGHE Solution, coupling via Tasking interface may increase. Mitigation: calibration parameters should be cached locally on Vehicle and updated asynchronously.

Risk: If evaluation period is dynamically reconfigured by Teleoperation with high frequency, network latency on Tasking input may degrade measurement accuracy. Mitigation: define evaluation period as a stable configuration parameter with infrequent updates.

Risk: If Safety Services must validate flow measurements for anomaly detection, bidirectional coupling may emerge. Mitigation: Safety Services should consume flow data via Telemetry (read-only), not participate in acquisition logic.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP1 correctly maps to FR by specifying the mechanism (continuous sensing + integration) to achieve the functional requirement (measurement and reporting of material throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time). The DP is logically sound: instantaneous flow rate acquisition via continuous sensing, integrated over an evaluation period, directly produces cumulative throughput. No logical gaps or contradictions detected.

diffusion_risks: MODERATE: Tasking interface (evaluation period parameters and flow measurement configuration) creates a dependency on external command authority. If Teleoperation or NGHE Solution frequently reconfigures evaluation periods with high latency, measurement accuracy and period boundary integrity may degrade. Mitigation: define evaluation period as a stable, infrequently-updated configuration parameter; cache locally on Vehicle.

MODERATE: If Safety Services requires real-time validation or anomaly detection on flow measurements, bidirectional coupling may emerge between Vehicle measurement logic and Safety Services. This violates the read-only consumption pattern. Mitigation: Safety Services must consume flow data via Telemetry interface only (unidirectional); do not embed safety validation logic in Vehicle's measurement acquisition.

LOW: If external calibration or reference data from NGHE Solution is required for flow measurement accuracy, coupling via Tasking interface increases. Mitigation: calibration parameters should be pre-loaded and cached locally on Vehicle; updates should be asynchronous and decoupled from real-time measurement cycles.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Vehicle module achieves excellent containment (0.92 score) and minimal diffusion (0.08 score). All core DP logic—continuous sensing hardware, signal conditioning, integration computation, and cumulative throughput calculation—resides within the Vehicle boundary. External interfaces (Telemetry out, Tasking in, Network Link inout) are clean and well-defined. NGHE Solution, Teleoperation, and Safety Services are correctly excluded from measurement acquisition ownership; they consume outputs or provide configuration only. The DP-to-IM mapping is tight and cohesive.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: HIGH. The Vehicle module is a physical and logical boundary with direct access to flow sensing hardware and real-time computation resources. Verification of the FR (reported productivity ≥ productivity_target over evaluation period) is feasible via: (1) direct measurement of instantaneous flow rate from onboard sensors; (2) integration of flow rate over the agreed evaluation period; (3) transmission of cumulative throughput via Telemetry interface to external systems for logging and validation. Acceptance criteria can be verified against sensor data and operational logs.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: HIGH. The FR is unambiguously allocated to the Vehicle module. The allocation is justified by: (1) physical co-location of sensing hardware on the vehicle; (2) requirement for low-latency, continuous real-time acquisition; (3) clear separation of concerns (Vehicle owns measurement physics; NGHE owns planning; Teleoperation owns remote command; Safety Services owns hazard detection). No ambiguity regarding which module is responsible for throughput measurement and reporting.

recommended_revisions: Formalize evaluation period as a stable configuration parameter with explicit versioning and infrequent update cadence. Define maximum allowable latency for Tasking input (evaluation period updates) to ensure measurement period boundary integrity.

Establish a read-only Telemetry consumption pattern for Safety Services: Safety Services shall consume flow measurements via Telemetry interface for anomaly detection or hazard alerting, but shall NOT participate in or modify the Vehicle's measurement acquisition logic.

Document calibration parameter management: specify whether calibration data is pre-loaded on Vehicle, updated via Tasking interface, or sourced from NGHE Solution. If sourced externally, define caching strategy and asynchronous update protocol to decouple from real-time measurement cycles.

Define Network Link reliability and latency requirements to ensure Telemetry output (instantaneous flow rate and cumulative throughput) reaches external systems with sufficient fidelity for productivity validation against acceptance criteria.

Add explicit acceptance test: verify that cumulative throughput reported by Vehicle over a representative evaluation period matches independent ground-truth measurement (e.g., manual volumetric or mass sampling) within acceptable tolerance (e.g., ±5%).

decision.justification: Gate results confirm FR-DP-IM coherence and high verification feasibility at the Vehicle module level. However, safety criticality (high) and the identified diffusion risks—particularly around evaluation period stability, Safety Services coupling, and calibration parameter management—warrant decomposition of the DP into sub-level FRs to explicitly address: (1) real-time flow sensing and signal conditioning; (2) integration and cumulative throughput computation; (3) evaluation period boundary management and configuration stability; (4) telemetry output and external interface integrity. This decomposition will clarify ownership of safety-critical measurement logic, decouple Safety Services consumption, and establish explicit sub-module boundaries for calibration and tasking inputs. Stopping at the current level risks embedding these concerns implicitly in a monolithic Vehicle measurement logic, reducing auditability and increasing integration risk with Safety Services.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Acquire instantaneous flow rate from onboard sensing hardware with continuous temporal resolution and signal fidelity sufficient to support integration-based throughput calculation.
FR1.2: Condition and validate acquired flow rate signals to remove noise, outliers, and sensor artifacts while preserving measurement accuracy within calibration tolerance.
FR1.3: Integrate instantaneous flow rate over a defined evaluation period to compute cumulative throughput (volumetric or mass units).

FR1.4: Manage evaluation period boundaries (start, end, duration) as a stable, versioned configuration parameter with explicit update protocol and local caching to ensure measurement period integrity independent of external command latency.
FR1.5: Apply calibration parameters (sensor offset, scale factor, environmental compensation) to raw flow measurements to achieve accuracy within acceptance tolerance (e.g., ±5% vs. ground truth).

FR1.6: Transmit instantaneous flow rate and cumulative throughput to external systems (Telemetry interface) with sufficient fidelity and reliability to enable independent productivity validation and Safety Services anomaly detection.

FR1.7: Enforce read-only consumption boundary: ensure that Safety Services and other external consumers access flow measurement data via Telemetry interface only; prohibit bidirectional coupling or modification of measurement acquisition logic by external modules.

revise.why: Current DP1 is logically sound but operates at a level of abstraction that obscures critical sub-concerns: signal conditioning, calibration, evaluation period stability, and Safety Services decoupling. Revise DP1 to explicitly decompose into sub-DPs addressing each FR1.x, with clear ownership boundaries and interface contracts. This will support the recommended revisions from the gate (formalize evaluation period, establish read-only Telemetry pattern, document calibration management) and reduce integration risk with Safety Services by making measurement logic modular and auditable.

assumptions: Evaluation period (duration, start/end triggers) is a stable, infrequently-updated configuration parameter that can be cached locally on Vehicle without real-time synchronization with Teleoperation or NGHE Solution.

Calibration parameters (sensor offset, scale factor, environmental compensation) are either pre-loaded on Vehicle or updated asynchronously via Tasking interface, decoupled from real-time measurement cycles.
Safety Services will consume flow measurements via Telemetry interface only and will not embed validation logic in Vehicle's measurement acquisition module.

Network Link provides sufficient reliability and latency to transmit Telemetry output (instantaneous flow rate and cumulative throughput) to external systems for productivity validation and Safety Services anomaly detection.

Onboard sensing hardware (flow meter, pressure transducer, or equivalent) is physically co-located on Vehicle and provides continuous, real-time flow rate acquisition with sufficient temporal resolution for integration-based throughput calculation.
Ground-truth measurement method (manual volumetric or mass sampling) is available for acceptance testing and calibration validation.
open_risks: MODERATE: Evaluation period boundary integrity depends on stable, locally-cached configuration. If Tasking interface forces frequent reconfiguration with high latency, measurement period boundaries may drift or overlap, degrading throughput accuracy. Mitigation: formalize evaluation period as versioned configuration with explicit update protocol and maximum allowable latency threshold.

MODERATE: Safety Services may attempt to embed real-time validation or anomaly detection logic in Vehicle's measurement acquisition, creating bidirectional coupling and violating read-only consumption pattern. Mitigation: establish explicit interface contract requiring Safety Services to consume flow data via Telemetry only; enforce architectural review to prevent Safety Services participation in measurement logic.

MODERATE: Calibration parameter management is underspecified. If calibration data is sourced from NGHE Solution with variable latency or requires real-time updates, coupling increases and measurement accuracy may degrade. Mitigation: define caching strategy and asynchronous update protocol; pre-load calibration parameters on Vehicle; specify maximum allowable staleness for cached calibration data.

LOW: Signal conditioning and noise filtering logic may introduce latency or artifacts that degrade integration accuracy. Mitigation: specify signal conditioning requirements (filter type, cutoff frequency, phase lag) and validate against ground-truth measurement.

LOW: Network Link reliability may be insufficient to transmit Telemetry output with required fidelity. If Telemetry packets are dropped or delayed, external systems may not receive complete flow measurement data for productivity validation. Mitigation: define Network Link reliability and latency requirements; implement local buffering and retry logic on Vehicle to ensure Telemetry output integrity.
evidence_needed: Specification of evaluation period configuration parameter: format, versioning scheme, update protocol, maximum allowable latency for Tasking input, and local caching strategy.
Specification of calibration parameter management: source (pre-loaded, Tasking interface, NGHE Solution), update frequency, caching strategy, and maximum allowable staleness.

Interface contract between Vehicle and Safety Services: explicit statement that Safety Services consumes flow measurements via Telemetry interface only; prohibition on bidirectional coupling or modification of measurement acquisition logic.
Signal conditioning and noise filtering specification: filter type, cutoff frequency, phase lag, and validation against ground-truth measurement.
Network Link reliability and latency requirements: minimum packet delivery rate, maximum latency, and buffering strategy to ensure Telemetry output integrity.

Acceptance test plan: procedure for verifying cumulative throughput reported by Vehicle against independent ground-truth measurement (e.g., manual volumetric or mass sampling) within acceptable tolerance (e.g., ±5%).
Sensor hardware specification: flow meter type, measurement range, accuracy, temporal resolution, and environmental compensation requirements.

Safety criticality analysis: identification of failure modes in flow measurement acquisition, signal conditioning, calibration, and evaluation period management; assessment of impact on system safety and hazard detection; definition of safety-critical sub-modules requiring additional verification or redundancy.
